title: if, else if, and else Statements

explanation.title: Making Decisions in Java with if, else if, and else

codeExample.title: Student Grade Classifier
codeExample.explanation:
- `if (score >= 90)` — condition in parentheses; body in braces; both are required in Java
- `else if (score >= 80)` — evaluated only when all previous conditions were `false`
- `String grade, feedback;` — both variables declared before the if-chain; they must be assigned in every branch
- `grade = "B"; feedback = "Great work!";` — assigning inside a branch; Java's compiler verifies every branch assigns both variables
- `score >= 50 ? "PASS" : "FAIL"` — ternary; evaluates to `"PASS"` when condition is true, `"FAIL"` otherwise

exercise.title: Write a Cinema Ticket Price Calculator
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that determines cinema ticket price based on age. Use if/else if/else with these rules: under 5 = free (price 0), 5 to 12 = Rs 100, 13 to 17 = Rs 150, 18 to 59 = Rs 200, 60 and above = Rs 120. Declare int age = 25 and int ticketPrice. Assign ticketPrice in the branches. Print age and ticket price with labels.

Expected output with age 25:
Age: 25
Ticket Price: Rs 200
hints: 💡 Add else if (age <= 17) { ticketPrice = 150; } after the age <= 12 branch.
💡 Add else if (age <= 59) { ticketPrice = 200; } for adults.
💡 The final else covers age 60 and above — no condition needed since all lower ages are already handled.

questions.question: What is the correct Java syntax to start an if statement?
questions.explanation: Java's if statement syntax requires parentheses around the condition and curly braces around the body: if (condition) { ... }. Java is case-sensitive — IF is invalid. Unlike Python, there is no colon. The parentheses are not optional as they are in some other languages.

questions.question: What does the expression score >= 90 ? "A" : "B" evaluate to when score is 85?
questions.explanation: The ternary operator evaluates the condition (score >= 90). Since 85 < 90, the condition is false, so the expression evaluates to the value after the colon — "B". The ternary operator is shorthand for a two-branch if/else and produces a value that can be assigned to a variable.
